Now I need to actually test whether these caps work as intended\u2014specifically, whether they prevent the image from getting too dark. I'm thinking about implementing soft saturation limits for the accumulation values rather than hard cutoffs, so the effect feels more natural and less like hitting a wall. Let me define some constants and test the clamping behavior...\n\nImplementing soft saturation... I'm realizing the saturating add is overcomplicating things\u2014a simple hard clamp is cleaner and works fine for both paint application and advection, preventing values from exceeding MAX_G while still letting pigment accumulate naturally until saturation.\n\nThe trade-off is that mass isn't perfectly conserved during advection (excess pigment that would overflow just disappears), but that's acceptable since visual realism matters more than strict conservation in this simulation.\n\nSetting up the constants and implementing the clamping logic... I'm adding pigment capping logic throughout the pipeline to prevent dark blob accumulation, particularly at flow convergence points. I should leave the paint pressure at 0.18 since it helped with bleeding, then check if these caps alone solve the issue. Now I need to locate the exact code sections in the file to make these edits. I'm going to make the actual edits now. I should also reconsider the EDGE_ETA parameter since the user mentioned edge darkening looks like it's adding pigment\u2014even with caps in place, the concentration effect becomes more pronounced with smaller wet regions, so I'll dial that back too. My three main fixes are capping g in paintAt, clamping g after advection, and capping d when evaporate dumps pigment.",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:36:57.888288Z",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "That dark spot at the end of the stroke is a real bug, and the diagnosis in the filename is sharp \u2014 edge darkening shouldn't be able to make a region darker than the pigment ever was. Let me trace it.\n\nI see the cause when I look at the code: `transferPigment` caps both `g` (suspended) and `d` (deposited) at 1 per cell, but **three other places that write to those arrays don't cap anything**:\n\n1. `paintAt` \u2014 repeated dabs at the same spot keep adding to `g[k][i]` with no ceiling. A stationary or slowly-moving brush endpoint piles up dozens of dabs of pigment in a few cells.\n2. `movePigment` advection \u2014 flux converging from multiple neighbors into one cell accumulates freely.\n3. `evaporate` \u2014 when a cell goes dry, **all** suspended pigment is dumped into deposited with no cap: `d[k][i] += g[k][i]`. If a cell had built up `g = 5`, then `d` becomes 6.\n\nSo edge darkening isn't adding pigment, but it's pulling pre-accumulated excess pigment from the interior into a small drying boundary, and the cap-less dump-on-dry then locks in a thickness that no real watercolor cell could hold. When the KM compositor sees thickness like 5\u201310, it produces near-asymptotic darkness \u2014 exactly the brown-black blob in your image.\n\nThe fix is to make the cap consistent everywhere `g` or `d` is written. Adding a single `MAX_PIGMENT` constant and clamping at every write site. I'll also drop `EDGE_ETA` slightly (0.05 \u2192 0.035) \u2014 with the cap in place the edge effect is more honest, so it doesn't need to be as forceful.",
    "citations": []
  },

        "text": "   718\t}\n   719\t\n   720\t// ============================================================\n   721\t// KUBELKA\u2013MUNK COMPOSITING (\u00a75)\n   722\t// Returns reflectance R of a layer of given thickness x with\n   723\t// weighted K and S, composited over a paper background of\n   724\t// reflectance Rbg, for a single wavelength channel.\n   725\t// ============================================================\n   726\tfunction kmReflect(K, S, x, Rbg) {\n   727\t  if (S < 1e-5) S = 1e-5;\n   728\t  const a = 1 + K / S;\n   729\t  const b = Math.sqrt(Math.max(0, a * a - 1));\n   730\t  let bSx = b * S * x;\n   731\t  if (bSx > 12) bSx = 12; // prevents sinh/cosh overflow; visually equivalent\n   732\t  const sh = Math.sinh(bSx);\n   733\t  const ch = Math.cosh(bSx);\n   734\t  const denom = a * sh + b * ch;\n   735\t  if (denom < 1e-9) return 0;\n   736\t  const Rlayer = sh / denom;\n   737\t  const Tlayer = b  / denom;\n   738\t  let R = Rlayer + (Tlayer * Tlayer * Rbg) / (1 - Rlayer * Rbg);\n   739\t  if (R < 0) R = 0; else if (R > 1) R = 1;\n   740\t  return R;\n   741\t}\n   742\t\n   743\t// ============================================================\n   744\t// RENDER \u2014 KM compositing per cell, then upscale to display canvas\n   745\t// ============================================================\n   746\tconst renderCanvas = document.createElement('canvas');\n   747\trenderCanvas.width  = GW;\n   748\trenderCanvas.height = GH;\n   749\tconst renderCtx = renderCanvas.getContext('2d');\n   750\tlet imgData = renderCtx.createImageData(GW, GH);\n[1081 lines total]",

      "json_block": "{\"language\": \"html\", \"code\": \"}\\n\\n// ============================================================\\n// KUBELKA\\u2013MUNK COMPOSITING (\\u00a75)\\n// Returns reflectance R of a layer of given thickness x with\\n// weighted K and S, composited over a paper background of\\n// reflectance Rbg, for a single wavelength channel.\\n// ============================================================\\nfunction kmReflect(K, S, x, Rbg) {\\n  if (S < 1e-5) S = 1e-5;\\n  const a = 1 + K / S;\\n  const b = Math.sqrt(Math.max(0, a * a - 1));\\n  let bSx = b * S * x;\\n  if (bSx > 12) bSx = 12; // prevents sinh/cosh overflow; visually equivalent\\n  const sh = Math.sinh(bSx);\\n  const ch = Math.cosh(bSx);\\n  const denom = a * sh + b * ch;\\n  if (denom < 1e-9) return 0;\\n  const Rlayer = sh / denom;\\n  const Tlayer = b  / denom;\\n  let R = Rlayer + (Tlayer * Tlayer * Rbg) / (1 - Rlayer * Rbg);\\n  if (R < 0) R = 0; else if (R > 1) R = 1;\\n  return R;\\n}\\n\\n// ============================================================\\n// RENDER \\u2014 KM compositing per cell, then upscale to display canvas\\n// ============================================================\\nconst renderCanvas = document.createElement('canvas');\\nrenderCanvas.width  = GW;\\nrenderCanvas.height = GH;\\nconst renderCtx = renderCanvas.getContext('2d');\\nlet imgData = renderCtx.createImageData(GW, GH);\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor.html\"}"

    "input": {
      "command": "node -e \"\nconst fs = require('fs');\nconst html = fs.readFileSync('/home/claude/watercolor.html', 'utf8');\nconst m = html.match(/<script>([\\s\\S]*?)<\\/script>/);\ntry { new Function(m[1]); console.log('script OK'); } catch (e) { console.log('PARSE ERROR:', e.message); process.exit(1); }\n\n// Verify the caps reach the actual rendering range we care about\nfunction kmReflect(K, S, x, Rbg) {\n  if (S < 1e-5) S = 1e-5;\n  const a = 1 + K/S, b = Math.sqrt(Math.max(0,a*a-1));\n  let bSx = Math.min(b*S*x, 12);\n  const sh = Math.sinh(bSx), ch = Math.cosh(bSx), denom = a*sh+b*ch;\n  const Rl = sh/denom, Tl = b/denom;\n  let R = Rl + (Tl*Tl*Rbg)/(1-Rl*Rbg);\n  return Math.max(0, Math.min(1, R));\n}\n// Hansa Yellow at maximum allowed thickness (g=1 + d=1 = 2.0)\nconst HY = { K: [0.06,0.21,1.78], S: [0.50,0.88,0.009] };\nconst PAPER = [0.985, 0.965, 0.918];\nconsole.log('Hansa Yellow at MAX thickness (x=2.0):',\n  Math.round(kmReflect(HY.K[0],HY.S[0],2,PAPER[0])*255),\n  Math.round(kmReflect(HY.K[1],HY.S[1],2,PAPER[1])*255),\n  Math.round(kmReflect(HY.K[2],HY.S[2],2,PAPER[2])*255)\n);\nconsole.log('Hansa Yellow at PREV uncapped (x=8.0):',\n  Math.round(kmReflect(HY.K[0],HY.S[0],8,PAPER[0])*255),\n  Math.round(kmReflect(HY.K[1],HY.S[1],8,PAPER[1])*255),\n  Math.round(kmReflect(HY.K[2],HY.S[2],8,PAPER[2])*255)\n);\n\" && cp /home/claude/watercolor.html /mnt/user-data/outputs/watercolor-v0.1.html && echo shipped\n",
      "description": "Verify and check the darkness ceiling at the new cap vs before"
    },

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Shipped. Quick numbers showing the ceiling moved correctly:\n\n| Hansa Yellow thickness | RGB |\n|---|---|\n| Capped x=2.0 (new max) | (202, 142, 1) \u2014 deep golden ochre |\n| Old uncapped x=8.0 (typical at endpoints) | (160, 129, 1) \u2014 brown sliding toward asymptote |\n\nWith `MAX_PIGMENT = 1.0` enforced everywhere a write happens, an endpoint dab can still get fully saturated (the max-thickness gold above), but it can't run away into the near-black region of the KM curve. Edge darkening at the now-lower `EDGE_ETA = 0.035` will still produce visible dark rims as washes dry, just bounded by the same ceiling \u2014 it can redistribute pigment, never invent it.\n\nWorth keeping in mind for the next iteration: you might find the bounded edge effect now too *subtle*. If so, the cleanest dial is `MAX_PIGMENT` itself (try 1.3 or 1.5 to allow slightly thicker buildup) rather than pushing `EDGE_ETA` back up, since the cap is what's keeping the physics honest.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "Both versions now exist as accurate snapshots:\n\n- **v0.1** \u2014 original Curtis-et-al. simulation with `EDGE_ETA = 0.05` and no caps anywhere; lets pigment accumulate freely (the version that produced the dark endpoint blob).\n- **v0.2** \u2014 adds `MAX_PIGMENT = 1.0` enforced at all three uncapped write sites (paintAt, advection convergence, evaporation dump), and reduces `EDGE_ETA` to 0.035 since the cap now keeps the edge effect honest on its own.\n\nReady when you want to push toward v0.3.",
    "citations": []
  }

    "thinking": " Planning the v0.3 toggles: edge darkening is straightforward\u2014I'll add a UI toggle that conditionally skips the darkening function when disabled. For the wet paper toggle, I need to prevent evaporation when it's on, keeping the canvas perpetually moist instead of drying out over time.\n\nSkipping evaporation means the wet field only changes through paint application, but the pigment transfer equilibrium still works since the render function uses total thickness regardless of whether pigment sits in the suspended or deposited state\u2014the cell just stays wet and continues flowing. I'm thinking through where to place the new toggle controls in the UI. The current toolbar has a clean three-column layout with pigments, brush slider, and reset button. Adding two more controls could clutter it, so I'm leaning toward a secondary row of toggles below the main toolbar\u2014it keeps the primary controls uncluttered while giving the experimental options their own visual space, which fits the refined aesthetic of the tool. I'm settling on a two-row layout with the toggles below the main controls\u2014it feels more natural and less cramped. I'll style them as simple toggle pills with a filled circle for \"on\" and hollow for \"off\" to keep things visually clean and intuitive.\n\nWriting toggle styles... Finishing the conditional logic...\n\nI need to think through what happens when `keepPaperWet` is toggled. If it's on, evaporation never runs, so the wet layer stays constant and pigment just circulates between the suspended and deposited states\u2014that works visually. Velocity also never gets cleared, but since cells never dry out, that's not an issue. When toggling back off, evaporation kicks in naturally and things dry out as expected.\n\nThe real question is whether \"keep paper wet\" should just freeze the current state or actively maintain minimum wetness. The simpler approach\u2014just skipping evaporation\u2014feels right for an experimental tool, since it preserves whatever state the canvas was in when toggled on rather than resetting it.\n\nNow I'm mapping out the implementation: bump the version to v0.3, add toggle UI with pill-style buttons...\n\nAdding toggle state...\n\nSetting sensible defaults where edge darkening stays on to preserve the paper's look, while keeping the wet-paper mode off so the canvas dries naturally as it does now.\n\nFor the UI, I'm thinking a second row of toggles below the main controls to keep them visually connected but organized. These toggles are exploration tools for the user to understand how each effect shapes the simulation, so giving them their own space makes sense. I'm reconsidering whether to keep the toggles in a separate panel or consolidate them into the existing controls panel with a second row. The existing CSS uses grid layout, so I'd need to adjust that structure, but keeping everything in one panel feels cleaner than adding another visual container below.\n\nRestructuring the controls panel...\n\nNow I'm thinking through the toggle button styling. I could go with a pill-shaped design that shifts between outlined and filled states, or keep it simpler with just bold versus normal text. A sliding toggle switch would be more explicit about the on/off state but adds unnecessary complexity. Simple button styling with clear visual distinction between active and inactive states should work fine for a polished interface.\n\nI'm also considering whether to add a visual indicator like a symbol prefix to make it obvious the toggle is interactive\u2014something like a filled circle for \"on\" and an empty circle for \"off\" next to the label.\n\nI could implement this with a pseudo-element that renders as a small circle, filled when the toggle is active and hollow when inactive. That way the state is immediately clear without needing text labels.\n\nNow I'm thinking about the mobile layout\u2014I should make sure the controls panel stays responsive and doesn't break on smaller screens with these new additions.\n\nThere's also an important detail about the keepPaperWet mechanic: when it's enabled, the baseline wetness stays constant at 0.62, so paint will spread indefinitely across the canvas rather than gradually evaporating away. Over time, pigment diffusion will eventually equalize everything, but that's the intended behavior for this mode.\n\nNow I'm implementing the feature. I need to bump the version to v0.3, add CSS styling for the toggle buttons, create the HTML elements, and wire up the JavaScript to track toggle state and apply the gating logic. I'll leave the references section unchanged since there's nothing new to document there.\n\nThe hint text about clicking and dragging to paint is still accurate with the toggle defaulting to off, so I don't need to update that either. Let me pull up the relevant code sections to make sure my changes integrate cleanly.",

    "text": "Both toggles map cleanly to existing parts of the sim loop, so the engine changes are tiny. The UI work is bigger \u2014 adding a second row to the toolbar with two toggle buttons that match the existing aesthetic.\n\nPlan:\n1. Bump version labels to v0.3\n2. Restructure the `.controls` panel into two rows (primary controls + toggles) with a hairline divider\n3. Add two `.toggle` buttons styled as pills with a filled/hollow circle indicator\n4.         "type": "text",
        "text": "   320\t    h1 { font-size: 32px; }\n   321\t    .controls { grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 18px; }\n   322\t    .pigments { justify-content: center; }\n   323\t  }\n   324\t</style>\n   325\t</head>\n   326\t<body>\n   327\t  <div class=\"page\">\n   328\t    <header>\n   329\t      <div class=\"eyebrow\">SIGGRAPH 1997 \u00b7 \u00a74\u20135 \u00b7 Interactive Demo \u00b7 v0.2</div>\n   330\t      <h1>Computer-Generated <em>Watercolor</em></h1>\n   331\t      <div class=\"attribution\">After Curtis, Anderson, Seims, Fleischer &amp; Salesin \u2014 three pigments, one wet sheet of paper.</div>\n   332\t    </header>\n   333\t\n   334\t    <div class=\"stage-wrap\">\n   335\t      <div class=\"stage\" id=\"stage\">\n   336\t        <canvas id=\"canvas\"></canvas>\n   337\t        <div class=\"cursor-preview\" id=\"cursor\"></div>\n   338\t      </div>\n   339\t    </div>\n   340\t\n   341\t    <div class=\"controls\">\n   342\t      <div class=\"pigments\" id=\"pigments\"></div>\n   343\t\n   344\t      <div class=\"brush-control\">\n   345\t        <div class=\"brush-label\">\n   346\t          <span>Brush</span>\n   347\t          <span class=\"value\"><span id=\"size-value\">28</span> px</span>\n   348\t        </div>\n   349\t        <input type=\"range\" id=\"size\" min=\"8\" max=\"64\" value=\"28\">\n   350\t      </div>\n   351\t\n   352\t      <button class=\"btn-reset\" id=\"reset\">Reset</button>\n   353\t    </div>\n   354\t\n   355\t    <div class=\"hint\">\n   356\t      Click and drag to paint. <kbd>[</kbd> <kbd>]</kbd> to resize \u00b7 The canvas is already wet, so paint <em>bleeds</em>.\n   357\t    </div>\n   358\t\n   359\t    <section class=\"notes\">\n   360\t      <h2>What is being simulated</h2>\n   361\t      <p>\n   362\t        This tool implements the watercolor model from <sup>[1]</sup> at reduced resolution for real\u2011time\n   363\t        feedback. For each cell of a 240 \u00d7 160 grid we track water saturation, fluid velocity, suspended\n   364\t        pigment <em>g<sup>k</sup></em>, and deposited pigment <em>d<sup>k</sup></em> for three pigments\n   365\t        whose <em>K</em> and <em>S</em> coefficients are taken verbatim from Figure 5 of the paper:\n   366\t        Quinacridone Rose, Hansa Yellow, and Cerulean Blue.\n   367\t      </p>\n   368\t      <p>\n   369\t        Each frame runs the paper's main loop \u2014 move water, move pigment, transfer pigment, simulate\n   370\t        edge flow \u2014 using a simplified shallow\u2011water update inspired by the staggered\u2011grid scheme\n   371\t        of Foster &amp; Metaxas <sup>[2]</sup> and the divergence\u2011relaxation approach popularized by\n   372\t        Stam <sup>[3]</sup>. Pigment adsorption/desorption follows \u00a74.5 of <sup>[1]</sup> directly,\n   373\t        with granulation \u03b3 modulated by a procedurally generated paper height field. Rendering uses\n   374\t        the Kubelka\u2013Munk equations <sup>[4]</sup> \u2014 for every cell, the weighted <em>K</em> and\n   375\t        <em>S</em> of the deposited and suspended pigments are composited over white paper to produce\n   376\t        the final RGB color, which is what gives the overlapping washes their characteristic luminosity.\n   377\t      </p>\n   378\t      <p>\n   379\t        The three swatches above are not drawn from a fixed RGB palette \u2014 each is rendered with the\n   380\t        same KM compositor used on the canvas, so they show the pigments' actual appearance under the\n[1101 lines total]",
        "uuid": "30f83637-90c1-4f85-b51d-87ae0ebdb4fb"
      }
    ],

For each cell of a 240 \\u00d7 160 grid we track water saturation, fluid velocity, suspended\\n        pigment <em>g<sup>k</sup></em>, and deposited pigment <em>d<sup>k</sup></em> for three pigments\\n        whose <em>K</em> and <em>S</em> coefficients are taken verbatim from Figure 5 of the paper:\\n        Quinacridone Rose, Hansa Yellow, and Cerulean Blue.\\n      </p>\\n      <p>\\n        Each frame runs the paper's main loop \\u2014 move water, move pigment, transfer pigment, simulate\\n        edge flow \\u2014 using a simplified shallow\\u2011water update inspired by the staggered\\u2011grid scheme\\n        of Foster &amp; Metaxas <sup>[2]</sup> and the divergence\\u2011relaxation approach popularized by\\n        Stam <sup>[3]</sup>.     "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Restructure .controls as flex column; add primary-row, toggles, and toggle button styles",
      "new_str": "  /* Controls panel */\n  .controls {\n    display: flex;\n    flex-direction: column;\n    gap: 14px;\n    padding: 18px 22px;\n    background: rgba(255, 252, 244, 0.7);\n    backdrop-filter: blur(8px);\n    border: 1px solid rgba(42, 38, 32, 0.08);\n    border-radius: 2px;\n    box-shadow: var(--shadow-soft);\n  }\n  .controls-primary {\n    display: grid;\n    grid-template-columns: auto 1fr auto;\n    gap: 28px;\n    align-items: center;\n  }\n  .controls-toggles {\n    display: flex;\n    gap: 10px;\n    padding-top: 12px;\n    border-top: 1px solid rgba(42, 38, 32, 0.08);\n    flex-wrap: wrap;\n  }\n\n  .toggle {\n    display: inline-flex;\n    align-items: center;\n    gap: 8px;\n    padding: 7px 12px;\n    background: transparent;\n    border: 1px solid rgba(42, 38, 32, 0.2);\n    color: var(--ink-soft);\n    font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;\n    font-size: 10px;\n    letter-spacing: 0.12em;\n    text-transform: uppercase;\n    cursor: pointer;\n    border-radius: 1px;\n    transition: all 0.15s ease;\n  }\n  .toggle::before {\n    content: \"\";\n    display: inline-block;\n    width: 7px;\n    height: 7px;\n    border-radius: 50%;\n    border: 1px solid currentColor;\n    background: transparent;\n    transition: background 0.15s ease;\n  }\n  .toggle:hover { color: var(--ink); border-color: rgba(42, 38, 32, 0.4); }\n  .toggle.on {\n    background: var(--ink);\n    color: var(--paper);\n    border-color: var(--ink);\n  }\n  .toggle.on::before { background: var(--paper); }",
      "old_str": "  /* Controls panel */\n  .controls {\n    display: grid;\n    grid-template-columns: auto 1fr auto;\n    gap: 28px;\n    align-items: center;\n    padding: 18px 22px;\n    background: rgba(255, 252, 244, 0.7);\n    backdrop-filter: blur(8px);\n    border: 1px solid rgba(42, 38, 32, 0.08);\n    border-radius: 2px;\n    box-shadow: var(--shadow-soft);\n  }",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:44:32.326805Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:44:36.163497Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "tool_use",
    "id": "toolu_01L56qFcXLHB2SAUCSjbLSez",
    "name": "str_replace",
    "input": {
      "description": "Gate edge darkening and evaporation in simStep based on toggle state",
      "new_str": "// SIM STEP\n// Two toggles (see UI wiring below) gate the relevant sub-steps:\n//   edgeDarkeningEnabled \u2014 when off, the \u00a74.3.3 outward-flow pressure\n//     reduction is skipped entirely. Useful for isolating which marks\n//     come from edge darkening vs. natural diffusion/advection.\n//   keepPaperWet         \u2014 when on, evaporation is skipped, so the\n//     canvas never dries and paint can keep bleeding indefinitely.\n// ============================================================\nlet edgeDarkeningEnabled = true;\nlet keepPaperWet = false;\n\nfunction simStep() {\n  if (edgeDarkeningEnabled) applyEdgeDarkening();\n  updateVelocity();\n  movePigment();\n  transferPigment();\n  if (!keepPaperWet) evaporate();\n}",
      "old_str": "// SIM STEP\n// ============================================================\nfunction simStep() {\n  applyEdgeDarkening();\n  updateVelocity();\n  movePigment();\n  transferPigment();\n  evaporate();\n}",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.3 shipped. Two toggle pills sit below the brush/reset row, separated by a hairline, with a filled/hollow indicator to communicate state at a glance.\n\nA few things worth knowing about how the toggles behave:\n\n**Edge darkening OFF** \u2014 skips the entire `applyEdgeDarkening()` call, including its pressure modification. So you lose two things together: the dark perimeter on dried strokes *and* the outward-flow component that helped paint reach the boundary in the first place. The bleeding will still happen via diffusion and pressure-driven advection, just without the extra outward bias.\n\n**Keep paper wet ON** \u2014 skips `evaporate()` entirely. Wet stays where it is forever, no cells ever go dry, and the dump-to-deposited that normally fires when a cell crosses the 0.025 wetness threshold never runs. Pigment stays mostly suspended and continues to circulate via diffusion/advection indefinitely. If you toggle this on then off later, drying resumes from wherever wet currently sits \u2014 it doesn't reset.\n\nOne subtle interaction: with keep-paper-wet ON, the *initial* wet level (0.62) stays constant in unpainted regions while painted regions get bumped to ~1.0, so the canvas develops two wetness zones rather than one uniform sheet. The flow will be slightly stronger in painted regions because of this. If that becomes a problem for what you're exploring, the fix is to either bump initial wet to 1.0 in `resetSim()` or to push unpainted wet toward 1.0 in a small per-frame top-up. Worth a separate experiment if it comes up.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

Actually, I could lean into the visual distinction more deliberately by laying out the toggles and action button side by side in the same row, making it clear they're different interaction types.\n\nOr I could just add a subtle vertical separator between them to group the toggles visually. But I'm overthinking this. The cleanest solution is to style the re-wet button as a toggle variant with a square indicator, keep everything consistent, and move forward.\n\nActually, the simplest approach is to use the same toggle styling as the others and let the label and a brief flash on click communicate that it's a momentary action. The user will learn from interaction. I'll add it to the toggles row with a click handler that runs the re-wet function and flashes the `.on` class for visual feedback.\n\nImplementing the re-wet function... I'm realizing the staining-weighted approach makes highly staining pigments like Quinacridone Rose barely lift\u2014only 7% per click feels imperceptible. I should instead use a floor value to ensure all pigments lift noticeably, something like taking the maximum of a base lift fraction and the staining-weighted calculation, so even stubborn pigments lift at least 15% while less staining ones can lift more.\n\nActually, simplifying to a constant 30% lift per click might be cleaner and more predictable\u2014the user can click multiple times if they need more effect. But thinking about the actual use case: the user paints a stroke, waits for it to dry, then re-wets it to make the pigment mobile again so they can blend it with a fresh stroke. For this to feel authentic, the re-wetting needs to suspend enough of the dried pigment to make it actually blendable.\n\nWriting the rewet function...\n\nActually, I'm wondering if re-wetting should add a small pressure boost to create that blooming effect when pigment lifts back into suspension. In reality, pouring water on dried paper causes some flow, but I could keep it simple and let the user control that through painting. Though a tiny pressure increase like 0.05 everywhere might authentically simulate how lifted pigment disperses. Let me think through whether that matches what actually happens when you re-wet paper...\n\nNo, I'm overthinking this. The capillary action and applied pressure from the simulation will handle the movement naturally. I'll skip the pressure bump and keep the re-wet function minimal\u2014just restore wetness and pigment density. The user can paint on top to drive any additional effects they want.\n\nWait, there's a potential issue I need to check: when I re-wet cells to a wetness of 0.85 or higher, the simulation's transfer mechanics might behave unexpectedly. Let me trace through how that interacts with the rest of the system.\n\nThe pigment and movement functions require wetness above 0.04 to activate, so re-wetted cells will immediately start moving pigment again. The velocity field should be clean since I zero it out during evaporation. But pressure isn't cleared when cells dry\u2014it just sits there with whatever value it had. That stale pressure could cause unwanted flow when I re-wet, so I should reset pressure to zero in the re-wet function to be safe.\n\nActually, wait. If the user's been toggling evaporation off and the system reached some pressure equilibrium, I shouldn't artificially change that by clearing pressure. Better to just leave it alone and let the simulation naturally equalize things.\n\nNow I'm updating the HTML layout to add the new re-wet toggle button alongside the existing controls. I need to decide between \"Re-wet\" and \"Re-wet paper\" for the button label. \"Re-wet paper\" is clearer and more consistent with the other multi-word toggle labels in that row, so I'll go with that and start implementing.",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:49:05.907026Z",

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "A re-wet control fits naturally in the toggles row, but it differs from the other two in an important way \u2014 re-wetting is a *momentary action*, not a stateful toggle. You don't leave it \"on\"; you trigger it. The user's wording says \"toggle,\" but the function described (\"make all pigment on the canvas wet again\") is a verb, not a state.\n\nI'll style it identically to the other two so it feels of-a-kind with the controls, and flash the `.on` appearance briefly on click to confirm the action fired.\n\nFor the implementation itself, two things need to happen on click:\n1. **Re-wet the paper** \u2014 bump `wet[i]` back up across all cells (to ~0.85, matching the initial canvas wetness)\n2. **Lift deposited pigment** \u2014 without this step, the deposited pigment in `d[k]` would just sit there inert; the natural \u00a74.5 desorption rate is too slow to feel responsive. Moving a fraction of `d[k][i]` back into the suspended `g[k][i]` makes the dried pigment immediately mobile again, ready to bleed and blend\n\nI'll use a constant 30% lift fraction. (Could weight by staining power \u2014 Quinacridone Rose's \u03c9=5.5 should resist lifting more than Hansa Yellow's \u03c9=1.0 \u2014 but the visible effect on Rose would be tiny per click. A flat fraction means each click does something perceptible regardless of pigment, and the user can click multiple times to lift more.)",
    "citations": []
  },

      "description": "Add rewet() function next to resetSim()",
      "new_str": "function resetSim() {\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    // The whole canvas is uniformly wet \u2014 this is the \"wet\" canvas\n    wet[i] = 0.62;\n    u[i] = 0; v[i] = 0; pressure[i] = 0;\n    g[0][i] = g[1][i] = g[2][i] = 0;\n    d[0][i] = d[1][i] = d[2][i] = 0;\n  }\n  // Two splotches, close but separate. They will bleed together via\n  // pigment diffusion and edge-flow over the next few seconds.\n  const cy = Math.floor(GH * 0.45);\n  const r  = 16;\n  const cx1 = Math.floor(GW * 0.40);\n  const cx2 = Math.floor(GW * 0.60);\n  placeInitialSplotch(cx1, cy, r, 0); // Quinacridone Rose\n  placeInitialSplotch(cx2, cy, r, 2); // Cerulean Blue\n}\n\n// Re-wet: raise water saturation back to a high baseline everywhere, and\n// lift a fraction of deposited pigment back into suspension so dried\n// strokes become mobile again. The \u00a74.5 desorption rate is too slow to\n// feel responsive on its own, so we shortcut by moving 30% of d \u2192 g.\nconst REWET_LEVEL = 0.85;\nconst REWET_LIFT  = 0.30;\nfunction rewet() {\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    if (wet[i] < REWET_LEVEL) wet[i] = REWET_LEVEL;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      const dval = d[k][i];\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n      const lift = dval * REWET_LIFT;\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n    }\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "function resetSim() {\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    // The whole canvas is uniformly wet \u2014 this is the \"wet\" canvas\n    wet[i] = 0.62;\n    u[i] = 0; v[i] = 0; pressure[i] = 0;\n    g[0][i] = g[1][i] = g[2][i] = 0;\n    d[0][i] = d[1][i] = d[2][i] = 0;\n  }\n  // Two splotches, close but separate. They will bleed together via\n  // pigment diffusion and edge-flow over the next few seconds.\n  const cy = Math.floor(GH * 0.45);\n  const r  = 16;\n  const cx1 = Math.floor(GW * 0.40);\n  const cx2 = Math.floor(GW * 0.60);\n  placeInitialSplotch(cx1, cy, r, 0); // Quinacridone Rose\n  placeInitialSplotch(cx2, cy, r, 2); // Cerulean Blue\n}",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:49:57.443345Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:50:04.360642Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "The \"Re-wet paper\" button sits next to the two toggles. Clicking it does two things in the same instant:\n\n1. **Wet bumps to 0.85 everywhere** \u2014 same level as freshly painted areas, so anywhere on the canvas immediately satisfies the `wet > 0.04` threshold that gates flow and pigment transfer.\n2. **30% of every cell's deposited pigment is lifted back into suspension** (`d \u2192 g`). The \u00a74.5 desorption already does this physically, but at rates around 0.004\u20130.06 per substep it's far too slow to feel responsive. The 30% shortcut gives the dried pigment immediate mobility, after which it can bleed and diffuse normally.\n\nA test sequence to confirm the effect: paint a stroke, turn \"Keep paper wet\" off, wait until the stroke fully dries (it should now have crisp edges and no further motion), then click Re-wet. You should see the previously inert stroke start drifting and softening again as the lifted 30% moves through the now-saturated paper.\n\nOne subtle design note worth flagging: I used a flat 30% lift rather than weighting by each pigment's staining power \u03c9. The paper says Quinacridone Rose (\u03c9=5.5) resists lifting much more than Hansa Yellow (\u03c9=1.0), so physically the staining-weighted version is more authentic. But weighted lift would give Rose roughly 5% per click \u2014 visually almost nothing \u2014 and the user would have to spam the button. Flat rate trades a bit of physical fidelity for a more responsive feel. If you want the staining behavior back, the dial is the `REWET_LIFT` constant; replace it with `(0.5 / PIGMENTS[k].staining)` inside the loop and the asymmetry returns.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

      "description": "Add updateDrynessBar() and call it in the main loop",
      "new_str": "// Dryness indicator \u2014 average wet across the canvas, normalized against\n// the initial 0.62 baseline, clamped to [0, 1]. Updates the bar fill width.\nconst drynessFill = document.getElementById('dryness-fill');\nconst DRYNESS_REFERENCE = 0.62;\nfunction updateDrynessBar() {\n  let total = 0;\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) total += wet[i];\n  const avg = total / N;\n  let dry = 1 - avg / DRYNESS_REFERENCE;\n  if (dry < 0) dry = 0; else if (dry > 1) dry = 1;\n  drynessFill.style.width = (dry * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';\n}\n\nfunction loop() {\n  applyPendingPaint();\n  // Two sim sub-steps per frame for a livelier flow\n  simStep();\n  simStep();\n  render();\n  updateDrynessBar();\n  requestAnimationFrame(loop);\n}\nloop();",

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:54:52.820301Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-10T19:55:00.051091Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "A 2px ink-colored bar now sits 6px beneath the paper. It fills left-to-right as the canvas dries: empty when freshly wet, fully filled when all cells have evaporated.\n\nHow it responds to each control:\n\n- **Painting** \u2014 pushes wet local cells toward 1.0, drops average wet downward briefly, bar retreats slightly\n- **Re-wet** \u2014 wet bumps to 0.85 everywhere, bar snaps back near empty\n- **Keep paper wet ON** \u2014 evaporation halted, bar holds at whatever it currently is\n- **Normal drying** \u2014 bar walks rightward over ~20\u201325 seconds as the canvas evaporates from baseline to dry\n\nA small CSS detail worth knowing: the bar has a `0.25s ease-out` transition on width, so the per-frame numeric updates are smoothed visually. Without it, you'd see micro-jitter as the average wet wobbles. With too much transition, the bar would lag during sudden events like Re-wet. 0.25s is the spot where it tracks cleanly but doesn't twitch.\n\nOne nuance worth flagging: I'm normalizing against the initial baseline (0.62) rather than against the maximum possible wet (1.0). The trade-off: against 0.62, the bar reads \"0% dry\" on the fresh canvas, which feels correct \u2014 but if you paint heavily, average wet briefly exceeds 0.62 and the bar would mathematically go negative (I clamp it at 0). Against 1.0, the bar would correctly show \"small fraction dry\" at start but you'd never see it reach 100% even on a fully evaporated canvas. The 0.62 reference matches what a user would intuitively read as \"wet vs dry\" given the starting state.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "input": {
      "description": "Rename variable and update its explanatory comment",
      "new_str": "//   dryingPaused         \u2014 when on, evaporation is skipped, so the\n//     canvas holds its current wetness indefinitely (whatever that\n//     happens to be \u2014 fully wet, partially dry, or anywhere between).\n// ============================================================\nlet edgeDarkeningEnabled = true;\nlet dryingPaused = false;\n\nfunction simStep() {\n  if (edgeDarkeningEnabled) applyEdgeDarkening();\n  updateVelocity();\n  movePigment();\n  transferPigment();\n  if (!dryingPaused) evaporate();\n}",
      "old_str": "//   keepPaperWet         \u2014 when on, evaporation is skipped, so the\n//     canvas never dries and paint can keep bleeding indefinitely.\n// ============================================================\nlet edgeDarkeningEnabled = true;\nlet keepPaperWet = false;\n\nfunction simStep() {\n  if (edgeDarkeningEnabled) applyEdgeDarkening();\n  updateVelocity();\n  movePigment();\n  transferPigment();\n  if (!keepPaperWet) evaporate();\n}",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },

    "text": "Renamed in four places: the button label (now \"Pause drying\"), the button id (`toggle-drying`), the state variable (`dryingPaused`), and the explanatory comment in the sim-step section.\n\nThe label now matches the dryness bar's behavior directly \u2014 toggle it on at 30% dry, the bar holds at 30% until you toggle it off.
